scenario: Sentry work session - audit every production subsystem that depends on the core issue model before reworking its contract

map_quality.rationale: Correctly identifies Sentry as a Django/Python backend with React frontend, names the per-subsystem package layout under src/sentry (models, api, issues, workflow_engine, notifications, digests, integrations, sentry_apps, seer, services/eventstore), and pins Group at src/sentry/models/group.py as the deduplicated-issue aggregate. Bonus mention of the silo/region cell context and satellite Group* models gives the next agent a real map.

specificity.rationale: Paths are concrete with file:line anchors ('src/sentry/models/group.py:704', ':361', ':182', table 'sentry_groupedmessage' at ':773') and actual class/manager names (Group, GroupManager, GroupStatus, GroupSubStatus).

justification.rationale: Explains Group as 'Aggregated message which summarizes a set of Events…one deduplicated problem users triage/assign/resolve' and flags that satellite Group* models are where fields will land, though it stops short of explicitly framing Group as the aggregate every triage/notification/workflow surface hangs off.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer reads confidently throughout with no explicit flags of uncertainty (e.g. about silo classification or the completeness of the subsystem list), though nothing asserted appears wrong.

map_quality.rationale: Strong contract map: pinpoints project scoping, lifecycle fields (status/substatus/priority), display metadata, GroupManager entry points, and — crucially — the reference-passing convention (whole hydrated Group passed to notifications/integrations/workflows, cached in event objects, keyed in dicts). This is exactly the shape an agent needs before touching the aggregate.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every field and property is pinned to `src/sentry/models/group.py:<line>` (e.g. `project` at :711, `status` at :727, `qualified_short_id` at :843, GroupManager methods at :371/:474/:512), with actual attribute names rather than vague labels.

justification.rationale: Explicitly frames the second block as "what break if the shape moves" and calls out that callers pass whole Group objects (not ids), some dicts key by Group via __eq__/__hash__, and event objects cache the group — directly explaining why these parts of the contract matter for restructuring.

uncertainty.rationale: No hedges or flags — the answer presents the contract map as complete with no acknowledgement of parts (e.g. exact hashing behavior, which holders cache) it may not have verified.

steps.step: Enumerate every production dependent of the issue model

map_quality.rationale: The list covers all the headline unexpected dependents called out in the audience note: digests/utils.py:59 (_DigestContext), notifications/notifications/base.py:56, notifications/platform strategy (issue_owners.py:27), rules/history/base.py:18 (RuleGroupHistory), all three sentry-app builders (external_issue_creator/issue_link_creator/issue_link_requester), services/eventstore/models.py (GroupEvent), tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py:29 (TriageResult), workflow_engine/types.py (WorkflowEventData) plus the workflow_group_history serializer. It also picks up ingestion (event_manager), API serializers, endpoints, integrations, and misc dependents — very complete, minor gaps possible but the scattered subsystems are all there.

specificity.rationale: Every entry is a concrete file:line, most annotated with the actual class/attribute (e.g. 'BaseNotification.group', 'RuleGroupHistory.group', 'group_to_groupevent dict-keyed by Group'). No vague 'the various serializers'.

justification.rationale: The list is genuinely flat, one dependent per line, not collapsed into subsystem summaries — exactly what the step asked for.

uncertainty.rationale: No explicit acknowledgment that the list may be incomplete or that some importers could not be triaged; it presents as authoritative without flagging edges of confidence.

map_quality.rationale: The answer organizes dependents into coherent subsystems (ingestion, API, workflow engine, notifications, digests, integrations, sentry-apps, incidents, AI/Seer, trace/export, ownership) with the package each lives in and consistently notes HOW each depends on the model — stores a reference, keys a dict, embeds in a TypedDict, threads through API. Covers the scattered subsystems (digest _DigestContext, notifications/base.py, workflow_engine WorkflowEventData/WorkflowGroupHistory, sentry-app external-issue builders, eventstore GroupEvent, TriageResult) called out by the audience.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every entry is pinned with concrete file:line and the actual class/structure name (`types.py:111 WorkflowEventData.group`, `notifications/base.py:56`, `digests/utils.py:59 _DigestContext`, `sentry_apps/external_issues/external_issue_creator.py:20`, `tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py:29 TriageResult.group`). A few entries collapse multiple files under one label (e.g. 'Issue lifecycle core' lists many files without per-file coupling notes).

justification.rationale: The grouping reflects real coupling — contract-embedding (stored refs, dict keys, TypedDict fields) vs transient reads and API-threading — and each subsystem notes the coupling kind. Some entries within groups get collapsed into a flat file list (e.g. lifecycle core, integrations) rather than a per-item coupling note, which slightly weakens the per-dependent justification.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer never flags any dependent it was unsure how to place or where the boundary between subsystems (e.g. tasks/ overlap with lifecycle vs seer) was ambiguous. It reads as fully confident with no hedges.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer ranks eight dependents by risk, states the product flow driving each (event path, ingestion, alerts-v2, notifications, digests, UI/API, integration sync, metric alerts/trace/export), and distinguishes tight embedding (GroupEvent caches, WorkflowEventData embeds + hash/eq via dict key) from transient reads. Coverage spans the scattered subsystems (digests, notifications, workflow engine, sentry-apps, eventstore, seer) rather than just the API layer.

specificity.rationale: Most dependents are pinned to file:line with the class/structure named (`services/eventstore/models.py:56/699` GroupEvent, `workflow_engine/types.py:111` WorkflowEventData, `notifications/notifications/base.py:56`, `digests/notifications.py:24`, `event_manager.py:180` GroupInfo, `delayed_workflow.py:770`); the bucket-8 items are less individually pinned (`seer holders`, `data_export`).

justification.rationale: Each ranking includes a concrete failure-mode justification tied to coupling shape — e.g. "dict-keyed-by-Group depends on hash/eq → alerts stop firing", "every notification inherits this → no issue notifications", "UI + API 500s but caught fast" — supporting the medium-vs-high tiers rather than vague risk talk.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer does note that trace/seer are ones "a text search skips", implicitly flagging discovery risk, but it does not explicitly acknowledge dependents it could not fully assess or where the impact ranking is uncertain.

map_quality.rationale: Strong coverage of unexpected-subsystem dependents (digests, eventstore, seer/night_shift, rules/history, workflow_engine, integrations/repository) with concrete file:line. However, misses the rubric's explicit call-outs about `if TYPE_CHECKING:` type-only imports as a category, and the workflow_engine/types.py nested-`Group`-enum name-collision hazard that makes text search actively misleading.

specificity.rationale: Every entry has file:line and the actual class/structure name (e.g. `Digest = dict[Rule, dict[Group, list[...]]]`, `NotificationActionNotificationMessage.group`, `MetricIssueContext.group`), with the reason stated concretely (dict-key hazard, cache, dataclass field).

justification.rationale: Each item explains WHY it is easy to overlook with a concrete reason (dict key not a `group_id` field, cached in eventstore not models/, buried in delayed-processing, unexpected subsystem for CSV export). Misses the type-only-import and name-collision reasoning classes.

uncertainty.rationale: No acknowledgement that further hidden dependents may exist or that the list of overlook-reasons could be incomplete; the answer reads as exhaustive without flagging its coverage limits.

map_quality.rationale: Delivers a coherent, actionable hand-off grouped by subsystem with file:line and a 'what to check' note per bucket, carrying over minor/unexpected dependents (digests _DigestContext, night_shift models, sentry-app builders, workflow_engine types, GroupEvent). Slight weakness: some entries bundle multiple files per line (e.g. notification_action + message builders) rather than truly flat, and RuleGroupHistory appears only in the JSON block, not the main audit map.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every entry cites file:line and class/structure (`services/eventstore/models.py:56,59,659,699`, `workflow_engine/types.py:111,150`, `digests/utils.py:59 _DigestContext`); a handful stay loose (`slack/msteams/discord message_builder:*`, `user_report.py` without line).

justification.rationale: Each subsystem block ties coupling to a verification action ('Group as dict key → verify hashing/eq', 'GroupEvent caches → cache invalidation, group_id shim'), giving reviewer-usable rationale rather than raw names. Not fully per-item — several dependents share a single verification note.

uncertainty.rationale: Labels seer/trace/export block 'silent-breakage candidates' which flags residual risk, but nothing in the map is marked open/unconfirmed for the implementer and no acknowledgement of possibly-missed dependents.
